sourceMovie.title: Psycho
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Psycho works as a horror movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The horror and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Dressed to Kill
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Dressed to Kill (1980) operates in the same horror as Psycho, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The dark payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Psycho II
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Psycho II (1983) belongs to the same conversation as Psycho — both anchor their horror in thriller emotion that doesn't let go. The dark undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Goodnight Mommy
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Psycho's horror stayed with you, Goodnight Mommy (2022) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The thriller through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Memento
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Psycho excels in thriller, Memento (2000) doubles down on mystery to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Identity
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Identity (2003) is the natural next step after Psycho: same thriller DNA, different perspective. The tense streak and mystery elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: The Number 23
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Psycho who respond to its thriller will find The Number 23 (2007) immediately familiar. Both films share a mystery sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: Crooked House
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Psycho fans keep landing on Crooked House (2017) — the thriller is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Night Moves
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Night Moves draws from the same thriller well as Psycho, channeling mystery in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 1975 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Mr. Brooks
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Mr. Brooks (2007) captures the thriller appeal of Psycho without simply imitating it. The crime streak running through both films is what puts Mr. Brooks at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Totally Killer
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror appeal of Psycho — that dark edge, the comedy — translates cleanly into Totally Killer (2023). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

faq.question: What should I watch after Psycho?
faq.answer: Try Dressed to Kill, Psycho II, Goodnight Mommy—each shares DNA with Psycho while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Psycho?
faq.answer: Psycho (1960) is best described as Horror, Thriller, Mystery. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Psycho part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Psycho Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
